title: Samsung Prioritizes Profitability Over High-NA EUV Expansion as Foundry Competition Intensifies
gist: Samsung is delaying large-scale deployment of its two installed High-NA EUV systems to protect foundry profitability amid low utilization, even as Intel moves High-NA into volume production on 18A Panther Lake and TSMC extends its node roadmap through 2029 -- three foundries now pursuing distinctly different paths to leading-edge manufacturing.

insights.claim: Samsung Electronics has installed two of ASML's High-NA EUV systems but is delaying broad deployment of the technology, keeping the tools focused on research, process optimization, and pilot production rather than large-scale manufacturing.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Signals Samsung is prioritizing near-term foundry margin recovery over being first-to-scale on next-gen lithography, delaying a major capex cycle.
insights.quote: choosing to delay broad deployment of High-NA EUV lithography despite already installing two of ASML's latest systems

insights.claim: Samsung's foundry division believes the depreciation and operating costs of High-NA EUV tools currently outweigh the manufacturing benefits, particularly because foundry utilization remains below optimal levels.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Low fab utilization is the binding constraint on Samsung's foundry economics -- watch Samsung foundry utilization rates as the trigger for any High-NA ramp.
insights.quote: the company believes the substantial depreciation and operating costs associated with High-NA tools outweigh the immediate manufacturing benefits, particularly while foundry utilization remains below optimal levels
insights.locator: third paragraph, on Samsung's cost calculus

insights.claim: Samsung's slow-walking of High-NA EUV should be read as a deliberate strategy to restore foundry profitability before committing to another wave of large-scale capital investment, not as a retreat from advanced manufacturing.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: If Samsung foundry demand and margins improve, expect a rapid pivot to aggressive High-NA capacity activation rather than a permanent pullback.
insights.quote: it represents a strategic effort to improve financial performance before committing to another wave of large-scale capital investment

insights.claim: If foundry demand strengthens and production economics improve, Samsung is expected to be able to activate its already-installed High-NA EUV capacity more aggressively without needing fresh equipment lead time.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Samsung retains optionality -- its installed base means a demand-driven scale-up could happen faster than a from-scratch capacity build.
insights.quote: the company is expected to be well positioned to activate its installed High-NA capacity more aggressively

insights.claim: Advanced semiconductor manufacturing leadership is no longer defined simply by adopting the newest lithography equipment first, but by the timing of when it becomes economically sensible to deploy it at scale.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Reframes the competitive lens for foundry investors: track deployment economics and utilization, not just who owns the newest tools first.
insights.quote: advanced manufacturing leadership is no longer defined solely by adopting the newest equipment, but by deploying it when the economics make sense

insights.claim: High-NA EUV is one of the semiconductor industry's largest equipment expenditures, and each new process generation requires significantly higher capital investment, forcing chipmakers to justify the tool's return on investment through sufficient production volume and customer demand.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Capex intensity is rising per node -- foundry profitability increasingly hinges on securing committed volume before deploying High-NA at scale.
insights.quote: High-NA EUV represents one of the industry's largest equipment expenditures

insights.claim: High-NA EUV offers higher resolution and can reduce multi-patterning complexity versus standard EUV, which is the core technical case for its eventual adoption despite high upfront costs.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The resolution/multi-patterning benefit is the long-term pull factor that should eventually force broader industry adoption once economics align.
insights.quote: the technology offers higher resolution and can reduce multi-patterning complexity

insights.claim: ASML has confirmed its High-NA EUV platform has entered high-volume logic manufacturing, marking a commercial milestone that validates years of development for the technology.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: High-NA has crossed from R&D into commercial-scale logic production, a maturity signal for ASML's platform and its downstream foundry customers.
insights.quote: ASML recently confirmed that its High-NA EUV platform has entered high-volume logic manufacturing

insights.claim: Intel became the first semiconductor manufacturer to use High-NA EUV in volume production, applying it to selected layers of its 18A Panther Lake processors, demonstrating production-level readiness for the technology.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Intel's first-mover use of High-NA in volume on 18A is a concrete data point for its foundry/process-leadership narrative versus TSMC and Samsung.
insights.quote: Intel becoming the first semiconductor manufacturer to use the technology in volume production for selected layers of its 18A Panther Lake processors

insights.claim: TSMC has extended its process technology roadmap through 2029, adding new nodes A12, A13, and N2U, while pushing the timing of A16 production out to 2027, showing it is pacing High-NA-era scaling around manufacturing efficiency and customer requirements rather than rushing adoption.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: TSMC's roadmap extension and A16 timing shift are concrete node-by-node markers to track TSMC's Angstrom-era execution against Samsung and Intel.
insights.quote: TSMC has extended its process technology roadmap through 2029, introducing future nodes including A12, A13, and N2U while adjusting the timing of A16 production to 2027

insights.claim: Intel's early volume deployment of High-NA EUV, Samsung's cost-focused delay, and TSMC's roadmap-driven pacing together show there is no single winning path to semiconductor manufacturing leadership in the Angstrom era.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Investors should evaluate each foundry's strategy on its own execution/financial terms rather than assuming a single lithography-adoption race determines the leader.
insights.quote: illustrates that there is no single path to semiconductor leadership

insights.claim: Growing demand from AI processors, high-performance computing devices, and next-generation mobile chips is expected to make High-NA EUV an increasingly important manufacturing technology across the industry over time.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: AI/HPC/mobile chip demand is the long-run catalyst that should eventually pull all major foundries into broader High-NA adoption, even laggards like Samsung.
insights.quote: As AI processors, high-performance computing devices, and next-generation mobile chips continue driving demand for leading-edge silicon, High-NA EUV is widely expected to become an increasingly important manufacturing technology
